verses.text: These are the names of the children of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob, each man with his household: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin; Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

These are the names of the children of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob, each man with his household: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin; Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

These are the names of the children of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob, each man with his household: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin; Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.

These are the names of the children of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob, each man with his household: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin; Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.
All who came from Jacob's loins were seventy souls — and Joseph was already in Egypt. The book that will tell of a nation begins by counting a family.

Joseph dies, and all his brothers, and all that generation. But the children of Israel are fruitful and increase abundantly. They multiply, grow mighty — and the land is filled with them. One verse changes everything: the family has become a people.

Joseph dies, and all his brothers, and all that generation. But the children of Israel are fruitful and increase abundantly. They multiply, grow mighty — and the land is filled with them. One verse changes everything: the family has become a people.
Then a new king arises over Egypt — one who did not know Joseph. The name of the deliverer is forgotten in the palace. Political memory is short. One generation can erase what the last one knew.

He tells his people: behold, the children of Israel are more and mightier than we. Come, let us deal wisely with them — lest they multiply and join our enemies in war and leave the land. Fear of the foreigner has the same accent in every century.

He tells his people: behold, the children of Israel are more and mightier than we. Come, let us deal wisely with them — lest they multiply and join our enemies in war and leave the land. Fear of the foreigner has the same accent in every century.

So they set taskmasters over them to afflict them with hard labor; they build the storage cities Pithom and Raamses for Pharaoh. But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied. The Egyptians grew grieved at the sight of Israel. Oppression backfired in plain view.

So they set taskmasters over them to afflict them with hard labor; they build the storage cities Pithom and Raamses for Pharaoh. But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied. The Egyptians grew grieved at the sight of Israel. Oppression backfired in plain view.

The Egyptians make the children of Israel serve with rigor. They make their lives bitter with hard bondage — mortar, brick, every kind of field service — all done with cruelty. The word rigor sits heavy in the Hebrew. The chapter is naming the system.

The Egyptians make the children of Israel serve with rigor. They make their lives bitter with hard bondage — mortar, brick, every kind of field service — all done with cruelty. The word rigor sits heavy in the Hebrew. The chapter is naming the system.

The king of Egypt speaks to the Hebrew midwives — Shiphrah and Puah. Their names are recorded; Pharaoh's name is not. When you deliver Hebrew women, watch the birthing stones: if a son, kill him; if a daughter, let her live. Genocide is being arranged through the women who hold newborns.

The king of Egypt speaks to the Hebrew midwives — Shiphrah and Puah. Their names are recorded; Pharaoh's name is not. When you deliver Hebrew women, watch the birthing stones: if a son, kill him; if a daughter, let her live. Genocide is being arranged through the women who hold newborns.

But the midwives feared God and did not do what the king of Egypt commanded. They saved the baby boys alive. Two women — without an army, without protection — quietly refuse the most powerful man in the world.

The king calls them in: why have you done this? Why have you saved the boys? The midwives answer: because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women; they are vigorous, and give birth before we arrive. They tell a story that protects the babies and themselves.

The king calls them in: why have you done this? Why have you saved the boys? The midwives answer: because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women; they are vigorous, and give birth before we arrive. They tell a story that protects the babies and themselves.

Therefore God dealt well with the midwives. The people multiplied, and grew very mighty. Because the midwives feared God, He made them houses — He gave them families of their own. Faithfulness in hidden work is rewarded with hidden families.

Therefore God dealt well with the midwives. The people multiplied, and grew very mighty. Because the midwives feared God, He made them houses — He gave them families of their own. Faithfulness in hidden work is rewarded with hidden families.

Then Pharaoh commands all his people: every son born to the Hebrews you shall cast into the river; every daughter you shall save alive. The plan moves from the midwives to the entire nation. The Nile that gave Egypt life is to be made into a grave.
